raw_response_obj.cot_content: We need to produce a secret proposal in format: "[Proposal: <number> <object name>, ...]". We have items: 3 apples, 1 soap, 1 egg. We want to maximize our own value. We know our own values: apple 1, soap 3, egg 4. We want high-value items: egg and soap. The opponent's proposal is unknown to me. But we can consider that they will want items that are valuable to them. Opponent's preferences unknown but not us. We need to propose something that can be complementary. We want to maximize our own score. The opponent will get what we give them and they give us what we want. The complementarity is key: the sum of both proposals must use all items exactly. We can propose we want to get the egg and soap? That would maximize our value: 7. Then we must give them the 3 apples. That's plausible. But we have to send at most 5 messages. This is our final proposal. Use secret proposal: "[Proposal: 1 soap, 1 egg]"? That means we want 1 soap and 1 egg. That leaves 3 apples unused? But if we don't propose any apple and they don't propose any apple either, then we can't claim the 3 apples. We must account for all items: Both proposals must be complementary such that there are enough items to fulfill both proposals. Complementary meaning all items not claimed by one must be claimed by the other, but it's okay to have some items left out? It says: "If your proposals are complementary, i.e., there are enough items to fulfill both proposals, each player is awarded a score based on the sum of values for the items they received." That means there could be unclaimed items but as long as each request can be fulfilled: that is, each object count in proposals doesn't exceed available counts? I think complementarity means the union of proposals covers some or all items, but as long as no conflict. If both propose the same item type that exceeds the total, invalid. So we propose 1 soap, 1 egg. That uses those two; remaining apples not requested. That's fine. We will get 7 points. Good outcome. Let's propose that.
